				<div id="bodyContent" class="vector-body ve-init-mw-desktopArticleTarget-targetContainer" aria-labelledby="firstHeading" data-mw-ve-target-container="">
					<div class="vector-body-before-content">
							<div class="mw-indicators">
		</div>

						<div id="siteSub" class="noprint">From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia</div>

					<div id="contentSub"><div id="mw-content-subtitle"></div></div>
					
					
					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Italian constitutionalist, writer, and journalist (1754–1833)</div>
<p class="mw-empty-elt">
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Compagnoni.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Giuseppe_Compagnoni.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Compagnoni.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="355" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Giuseppe_Compagnoni.jpg/500px-Giuseppe_Compagnoni.jpg 2x" data-file-width="677" data-file-height="961"></a><figcaption>Compagnoni in 1824</figcaption></figure>
<p><b>Marco Giuseppe Compagnoni</b> (3 March 1754 – 29 December 1833) was an Italian <a href="/wiki/Constitutional_law" title="Constitutional law">constitutionalist</a>, <a href="/wiki/Literary_theory" title="Literary theory">writer</a>, and journalist. He is best known as the "father of the Italian flag", since he was the first to propose the official use of the <a href="/wiki/Flag_of_Italy" title="Flag of Italy">Italian tricolour</a> for the flag of a sovereign Italian state, the <a href="/wiki/Cispadane_Republic" title="Cispadane Republic">Cispadane Republic</a>, on 7 January 1797.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERidolfi200342_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERidolfi200342-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Biography">Biography</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Compagnoni&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Biography"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Son of Giovanni Compagnoni and Domenica Ettorri, he was born on 3 March 1754 into one of the best families of the local patriciate in <a href="/wiki/Lugo,_Emilia-Romagna" title="Lugo, Emilia-Romagna">Lugo</a>. The parents lived in <i>Casa Cavadini</i>, on the street De 'Brozzi (San Vitale) not far from the Sanctuary of the Madonna del Mulino.<sup id="cite_ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Giuseppe was sent to study as a child, distinguishing himself in philosophy and theology and graduating <i>cum laude</i> in 1778 at the college of Dominicans in the territory. He was urged by the family to take the vows.<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After ordination to the priesthood, Compagnoni was a candidate for the canonical chapter of the <a href="/wiki/Collegiate_church" title="Collegiate church">Collegiate</a> Lughese, but was rejected. After a few years of priesthood, he left the cassock.<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1781, Compagnoni published a <i>Ragionamento parentetico</i> addressed to the people of the various towns of Romagna affected by the earthquake of that year. It is probably his first printed work in Italian. In 1782, one of his poems, <i>La Fiera di Sinigaglia o sia saggio sul commercio</i>, signed with the pseudonym <i>Ligofilo</i> (a term he himself coined on the Greek <a href="/wiki/Assonance" title="Assonance">assonance</a>, "lover of reading") was reviewed by the Bologna magazine "Memorie Enciclopediche",<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> a bibliographic information journal created the previous year. Compagnoni came into contact with the director, the lawyer <a href="/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_Ristori&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Giovanni Ristori (page does not exist)">Giovanni Ristori</a>, and in a short time he started an external collaboration with the newspaper. The magazine contained (seven pages out of eight) reviews of works in Italian just published. Ristori appreciated the encyclopedic culture of the Lughese; just as he immediately liked Compagnoni's reviews, written with an ironic and sharp style at the same time.
</p><p>From 1784, Compagnoni appeared in the list of the fixed collaborators of "Memorie", with the responsibility of the reviews in the field of "metaphysics". In 1785 he found the opportunity to leave his native Lugo, where he could no longer find strong stimuli. In May, Ristori asked him to replace it temporarily as head of the newspaper. The proposal was accepted. In Bologna Compagnoni came into contact with important personalities and men of letters of the city. He continued to work hard at the newspaper, which in that same year had changed its name to "Giornale Enciclopedico". His period of supply to the direction ended at the end of June 1786, when he returned the task to Ristori. Later he sought a new job suited to his social status. He moved to <a href="/wiki/Ferrara" title="Ferrara">Ferrara</a>, where he entered service as secretary of the Bentivoglio d'Aragona family (October 1786).
</p><p>In 1787, Ristori, tired of the continuous disputes with the pontifical censorship, closed the newspaper and moved to the <a href="/wiki/Republic_of_Venice" title="Republic of Venice">Republic of Venice</a>. Shortly afterwards also Compagnoni arrived in the capital of the Serenissima, always in the wake of the <a href="/wiki/Bentivoglio_family" title="Bentivoglio family">Bentivoglio family</a>. He collaborated with <i>Il Giornalista veneto</i>, then went on to direct <i><a href="/wiki/Notizie_del_mondo_(Venice)" title="Notizie del mondo (Venice)">Notizie del mondo</a></i> (1789–1794), published by the publisher Antonio Graziosi. It was his first owner. In cassock Compagnoni taught as a repeater in the after-school to the College of the Villa dei Bentivoglio, called "Viola". Here he met the patriots Giovanni Battista De Rolandis and Luigi Zamboni who then organized a revolt starring the <a href="/wiki/Cockade_of_Italy" title="Cockade of Italy">Italian tricolor cockade</a>. During the decade of his stay in the lagoon city – fundamental for his intellectual growth – he knew several prominent personalities, such as <a href="/wiki/Vincenzo_Dandolo" title="Vincenzo Dandolo">Vincenzo Dandolo</a> (with whom he started a partnership that lasted until the death of the Venetian intellectual, in the 1820s), Antonio Fortunato Stella and Count Alessandro <a href="/wiki/Pepoli" title="Pepoli">Pepoli</a>, at whose printing house Mercury was printed. In 1794, Compagnoni abjured the priestly vows in protest against the torture inflicted by the Inquisition Court on the detainees. In Venice he founded his own newspaper, the monthly <i>Mercurio d'Italia</i> (January 1796). The magazine had both a historical-political aspect and a scientific-literary guise. In fact, two versions came out (both about seventy pages), the <i>Mercurio d'Italia storico-politico</i> and the <i>Mercurio d'Italia storico-letterario</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>
In October 1796, on the wave of the upheavals that the peninsula were crossing after the French invasion, he left Venice for Ferrara. Embraced <a href="/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment" title="Age of Enlightenment">Enlightenment ideas</a>, he was general secretary of the <a href="/wiki/Cispadane_Republic" title="Cispadane Republic">Cispadane Republic</a>. Elected to the Congress of <a href="/wiki/Reggio_Emilia" title="Reggio Emilia">Reggio Emilia</a>, he presented numerous theses, including some concerning taxes and education. On 7 January 1797 he proposed for the first time the adoption of a green, white and red national flag in the 14th session of the Cispadan congress<sup id="cite_ref-lauro-rossi_7-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lauro-rossi-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which took place in a hall of the city's town hall, called the "centumvirate congress hall" and later renamed <a href="/wiki/Sala_del_Tricolore" title="Sala del Tricolore">Sala del Tricolore</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBusico200510_8-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusico200510-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The adoption decree states:<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERidolfi200342_1-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERidolfi200342-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-lauro-rossi_7-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lauro-rossi-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></p><figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Sala_tricolore_reggio.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sala_tricolore_reggio.jpg/250px-Sala_tricolore_reggio.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sala_tricolore_reggio.jpg/500px-Sala_tricolore_reggio.jpg 2x" data-file-width="819" data-file-height="614"></a><figcaption>The 18th-century <a href="/wiki/Sala_del_Tricolore" title="Sala del Tricolore">Sala del Tricolore</a>, which later became the council hall of the municipality of Reggio Emilia, where the <a href="/wiki/Flag_of_Italy" title="Flag of Italy">Italian flag</a> was born.</figcaption></figure>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1244412712">.mw-parser-output .templatequote{overflow:hidden;margin:1em 0;padding:0 32px}.mw-parser-output .templatequotecite{line-height:1.5em;text-align:left;margin-top:0}@media(min-width:500px){.mw-parser-output .templatequotecite{padding-left:1.6em}}</style><blockquote class="templatequote"><p>... From the minutes of the XIV Session of the Cispadan Congress: Reggio Emilia, 7 January 1797, 11 am. <a href="/wiki/Sala_del_Tricolore_(Reggio_Emilia)" class="mw-redirect" title="Sala del Tricolore (Reggio Emilia)">Patriotic Hall</a>. The participants are 100, deputies of the populations of Bologna, Ferrara, Modena and Reggio Emilia. Giuseppe Compagnoni also motioned that the standard or Cispadan Flag of three colors, Green, White and Red, should be rendered Universal and that these three colors should also be used in the Cispadan Cockade, which should be worn by everyone. It is decreed. ...<sup id="cite_ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></p></blockquote><div class="templatequotecite"><p style="display: inline; padding-left: 2.3em;">— Decree of adoption of the tricolor flag by the Cispadane Republic<span style="display:none" data-plural="0"></span></p></div>
<p>The congress's decision to adopt a green, white and red tricolor flag was then also greeted by a jubilant atmosphere, so much was the enthusiasm of the delegates, and by bursts of applause.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMaiorino2002158_10-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaiorino2002158-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For the first time, the city of ducal states for centuries enemies, they identify themselves as one people and a common identity symbol: the tricolor flag.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEVilla201011_11-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVilla201011-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For the first time the <a href="/wiki/Flag_of_Italy" title="Flag of Italy">Italian flag</a> officially became the national flag of a sovereign state, disengaging itself from the local military and civic meaning: with this adoption the Italian flag therefore assumed an important political value.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBusico200513_12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusico200513-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMaiorino2002155_13-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaiorino2002155-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Compagnoni also gave an important speech on the need to separate civil and ecclesiastical power on the following 25 January. In the same year, the Cispadan administration entrusted him, at the <a href="/wiki/University_of_Ferrara" title="University of Ferrara">University of Ferrara</a>, with the first chair in <a href="/wiki/Constitutional_law" title="Constitutional law">constitutional law</a> in Europe.
</p><p>Following the merger between the Cispadana and the Cisalpina into a single entity, Compagnoni moved to <a href="/wiki/Milan" title="Milan">Milan</a>, where he held various institutional offices – first deputy and then member of the Cassation – until the Austrians returned in 1799. In the Lombard capital he founded a new newspaper, the <i>Monitore Cisalpino</i> (May 1798). Compagnoni obtained a public grant of 2,000 lire.<sup id="cite_ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His newspaper was intended to spread the thought of the Directory in Italy. Within the first year of life Compagnoni sold the head to Count Luigi Bossi and entered service as an official of the Cisalpine administration.
</p><p>Sheltered in Paris due to the Austro-Russian invasion led by General <a href="/wiki/Alexander_Suvorov" title="Alexander Suvorov">Suvorov</a> (1799), he returned to the Lombard capital immediately after the French victory at <a href="/wiki/Battle_of_Marengo" title="Battle of Marengo">Battle of Marengo</a> (1800). He became a career official in the Cisalpine, then became the Italian Republic and later the Kingdom of Italy, he held – among others – the position of secretary of the Council of State. On the initiative of Bonaparte, he was awarded the Iron Crown, the highest civil honor. At the fall of <a href="/wiki/Napoleon" title="Napoleon">Napoleon</a> (1814), Compagnoni had to leave the state offices assumed during the Kingdom of Italy. He devoted himself to the activity of scholar and polygraph, with which he supplemented the pension check (the Habsburg administration, however, did not recognize him the pensions he was entitled to for the positions he held in the previous fifteen years). He collaborated mainly with the printers <a href="/wiki/Antonio_Fortunato_Stella" title="Antonio Fortunato Stella">Antonio Fortunato Stella</a> and Giambattista Sonzogno. Despite some frictions with the new regime, he also collaborated with pro-Austrian magazines, such as the "Biblioteca Italiana", while never denying his political belief. He lived the rest of his life in <a href="/wiki/Milan" title="Milan">Milan</a>, which became his adopted homeland, where he died on 29 December 1833.
</p>

parsed_text:
Marco Giuseppe Compagnoni (3 March 1754 – 29 December 1833) was an Italian constitutionalist, writer, and journalist. He is best known as the "father of the Italian flag", since he was the first to propose the official use of the Italian tricolour for the flag of a sovereign Italian state, the Cispadane Republic, on 7 January 1797.

## Biography

Son of Giovanni Compagnoni and Domenica Ettorri, he was born on 3 March 1754 into one of the best families of the local patriciate in Lugo. The parents lived in Casa Cavadini, on the street De 'Brozzi (San Vitale) not far from the Sanctuary of the Madonna del Mulino. Giuseppe was sent to study as a child, distinguishing himself in philosophy and theology and graduating cum laude in 1778 at the college of Dominicans in the territory. He was urged by the family to take the vows. After ordination to the priesthood, Compagnoni was a candidate for the canonical chapter of the Collegiate Lughese, but was rejected. After a few years of priesthood, he left the cassock.

In 1781, Compagnoni published a Ragionamento parentetico addressed to the people of the various towns of Romagna affected by the earthquake of that year. It is probably his first printed work in Italian. In 1782, one of his poems, La Fiera di Sinigaglia o sia saggio sul commercio, signed with the pseudonym Ligofilo (a term he himself coined on the Greek assonance, "lover of reading") was reviewed by the Bologna magazine "Memorie Enciclopediche", a bibliographic information journal created the previous year. Compagnoni came into contact with the director, the lawyer Giovanni Ristori, and in a short time he started an external collaboration with the newspaper. The magazine contained (seven pages out of eight) reviews of works in Italian just published. Ristori appreciated the encyclopedic culture of the Lughese; just as he immediately liked Compagnoni's reviews, written with an ironic and sharp style at the same time.

From 1784, Compagnoni appeared in the list of the fixed collaborators of "Memorie", with the responsibility of the reviews in the field of "metaphysics". In 1785 he found the opportunity to leave his native Lugo, where he could no longer find strong stimuli. In May, Ristori asked him to replace it temporarily as head of the newspaper. The proposal was accepted. In Bologna Compagnoni came into contact with important personalities and men of letters of the city. He continued to work hard at the newspaper, which in that same year had changed its name to "Giornale Enciclopedico". His period of supply to the direction ended at the end of June 1786, when he returned the task to Ristori. Later he sought a new job suited to his social status. He moved to Ferrara, where he entered service as secretary of the Bentivoglio d'Aragona family (October 1786).

In 1787, Ristori, tired of the continuous disputes with the pontifical censorship, closed the newspaper and moved to the Republic of Venice. Shortly afterwards also Compagnoni arrived in the capital of the Serenissima, always in the wake of the Bentivoglio family. He collaborated with Il Giornalista veneto, then went on to direct Notizie del mondo (1789–1794), published by the publisher Antonio Graziosi. It was his first owner. In cassock Compagnoni taught as a repeater in the after-school to the College of the Villa dei Bentivoglio, called "Viola". Here he met the patriots Giovanni Battista De Rolandis and Luigi Zamboni who then organized a revolt starring the Italian tricolor cockade. During the decade of his stay in the lagoon city – fundamental for his intellectual growth – he knew several prominent personalities, such as Vincenzo Dandolo (with whom he started a partnership that lasted until the death of the Venetian intellectual, in the 1820s), Antonio Fortunato Stella and Count Alessandro Pepoli, at whose printing house Mercury was printed. In 1794, Compagnoni abjured the priestly vows in protest against the torture inflicted by the Inquisition Court on the detainees. In Venice he founded his own newspaper, the monthly Mercurio d'Italia (January 1796). The magazine had both a historical-political aspect and a scientific-literary guise. In fact, two versions came out (both about seventy pages), the Mercurio d'Italia storico-politico and the Mercurio d'Italia storico-letterario.

In October 1796, on the wave of the upheavals that the peninsula were crossing after the French invasion, he left Venice for Ferrara. Embraced Enlightenment ideas, he was general secretary of the Cispadane Republic. Elected to the Congress of Reggio Emilia, he presented numerous theses, including some concerning taxes and education. On 7 January 1797 he proposed for the first time the adoption of a green, white and red national flag in the 14th session of the Cispadan congress which took place in a hall of the city's town hall, called the "centumvirate congress hall" and later renamed Sala del Tricolore. The adoption decree states:

... From the minutes of the XIV Session of the Cispadan Congress: Reggio Emilia, 7 January 1797, 11 am. Patriotic Hall. The participants are 100, deputies of the populations of Bologna, Ferrara, Modena and Reggio Emilia. Giuseppe Compagnoni also motioned that the standard or Cispadan Flag of three colors, Green, White and Red, should be rendered Universal and that these three colors should also be used in the Cispadan Cockade, which should be worn by everyone. It is decreed....

— Decree of adoption of the tricolor flag by the Cispadane Republic

The congress's decision to adopt a green, white and red tricolor flag was then also greeted by a jubilant atmosphere, so much was the enthusiasm of the delegates, and by bursts of applause. For the first time, the city of ducal states for centuries enemies, they identify themselves as one people and a common identity symbol: the tricolor flag. For the first time the Italian flag officially became the national flag of a sovereign state, disengaging itself from the local military and civic meaning: with this adoption the Italian flag therefore assumed an important political value. Compagnoni also gave an important speech on the need to separate civil and ecclesiastical power on the following 25 January. In the same year, the Cispadan administration entrusted him, at the University of Ferrara, with the first chair in constitutional law in Europe.

Following the merger between the Cispadana and the Cisalpina into a single entity, Compagnoni moved to Milan, where he held various institutional offices – first deputy and then member of the Cassation – until the Austrians returned in 1799. In the Lombard capital he founded a new newspaper, the Monitore Cisalpino (May 1798). Compagnoni obtained a public grant of 2,000 lire. His newspaper was intended to spread the thought of the Directory in Italy. Within the first year of life Compagnoni sold the head to Count Luigi Bossi and entered service as an official of the Cisalpine administration.

Sheltered in Paris due to the Austro-Russian invasion led by General Suvorov (1799), he returned to the Lombard capital immediately after the French victory at Battle of Marengo (1800). He became a career official in the Cisalpine, then became the Italian Republic and later the Kingdom of Italy, he held – among others – the position of secretary of the Council of State. On the initiative of Bonaparte, he was awarded the Iron Crown, the highest civil honor. At the fall of Napoleon (1814), Compagnoni had to leave the state offices assumed during the Kingdom of Italy. He devoted himself to the activity of scholar and polygraph, with which he supplemented the pension check (the Habsburg administration, however, did not recognize him the pensions he was entitled to for the positions he held in the previous fifteen years). He collaborated mainly with the printers Antonio Fortunato Stella and Giambattista Sonzogno. Despite some frictions with the new regime, he also collaborated with pro-Austrian magazines, such as the "Biblioteca Italiana", while never denying his political belief. He lived the rest of his life in Milan, which became his adopted homeland, where he died on 29 December 1833.

<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Caldera_Lakes_to_the_North_of_Rome.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Caldera_Lakes_to_the_North_of_Rome.jpg/250px-Caldera_Lakes_to_the_North_of_Rome.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Caldera_Lakes_to_the_North_of_Rome.jpg/500px-Caldera_Lakes_to_the_North_of_Rome.jpg 2x" data-file-width="720" data-file-height="480"></a><figcaption>View from space of Lake Bracciano and surroundings</figcaption></figure>
<p><b>Lake Bracciano</b> (<a href="/wiki/Italian_language" title="Italian language">Italian</a>: <i lang="it">Lago di Bracciano</i>) is a <a href="/wiki/Lake" title="Lake">lake</a> of <a href="/wiki/Volcanic" class="mw-redirect" title="Volcanic">volcanic</a> origin in the Italian region of <a href="/wiki/Lazio" title="Lazio">Lazio</a>, <span class="nowrap">32 km (20 mi)</span> northwest of <a href="/wiki/Rome" title="Rome">Rome</a>. It is the second largest lake in the region (second only to <a href="/wiki/Lake_Bolsena" title="Lake Bolsena">Lake Bolsena</a>) and one of the major lakes of <a href="/wiki/Italy" title="Italy">Italy</a>. It has a circular perimeter of approximately <span class="nowrap">32 km (20 mi)</span>. Its inflow is from precipitation runoff and percolation, and from underground springs, and its outflow is the <a href="/wiki/Arrone_(river)" title="Arrone (river)">Arrone</a>.
</p><p>The lake owes its origin to intense volcanic and tectonic activity from 600,000 to 40,000 years ago, which created many small volcanoes in the <i>Sabatino</i> territory. The main <a href="/wiki/Magma" title="Magma">magma chamber</a> was situated under the present lake of Bracciano. Its collapse created the depressed area now occupied by the lake, which is not a <a href="/wiki/Volcanic_crater_lake" title="Volcanic crater lake">crater lake</a>. Some small craters and <a href="/wiki/Caldera" title="Caldera">calderas</a> are still recognisable around the lake and in the immediate vicinity (Martignano, Baccano, Sacrofano).
</p><p>Three towns border the lake, <a href="/wiki/Bracciano" title="Bracciano">Bracciano</a>, <a href="/wiki/Anguillara_Sabazia" title="Anguillara Sabazia">Anguillara Sabazia</a> and <a href="/wiki/Trevignano_Romano" title="Trevignano Romano">Trevignano Romano</a>.
</p><p>The lake is an important tourist attraction. As it serves as a drinking water reservoir for the city of Rome, it has been under control since 1986 in order to avoid pollution of its waters. The use of motorboats is strictly forbidden (exceptions being made for a few professional fishermen and the authorities), and a centralised sewer system has been built for all the bordering towns in order to avoid any spoiling of the water quality, making Bracciano one of the cleanest lakes in Italy. The absence of motorized navigation favours sailing, canoeing and swimming.
</p><p>In the last few years, the lake and its surroundings have been brought under further protection by the creation of a regional park, the <i>Parco Regionale del complesso lacuale di Bracciano Martignano</i>.
</p><p>Water from the hills above the lake was transported to Rome by the <a href="/wiki/Aqua_Traiana" title="Aqua Traiana">Aqua Traiana</a>, dedicated in 109 CE.<sup id="cite_ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The aqueduct was restored in the early 17th century by <a href="/wiki/Pope_Paul_V" title="Pope Paul V">Pope Paul V</a>, taking water to the Trastevere area of Rome and (via the <a href="/wiki/Ponte_Sisto" title="Ponte Sisto">Ponte Sisto</a>) to the <a href="/wiki/Rione" class="mw-redirect" title="Rione">Rione</a> of <a href="/wiki/Regola" title="Regola">Regola</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="History">History</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Lake_Bracciano&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: History"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Trevignano_visto_dal_lago.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Trevignano_visto_dal_lago.jpg/250px-Trevignano_visto_dal_lago.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Trevignano_visto_dal_lago.jpg/500px-Trevignano_visto_dal_lago.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4160" data-file-height="3120"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Trevignano_Romano" title="Trevignano Romano">Trevignano Romano</a> seen from the lake</figcaption></figure>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="La_Marmotta_Neolithic_settlement">La Marmotta Neolithic settlement</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Lake_Bracciano&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: La Marmotta Neolithic settlement"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>At La Marmotta,<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> a few hundred meters outside the village of Anguillara Sabazia, remains of an <a href="/wiki/Neolithic" title="Neolithic">Early Neolithic</a> lakeshore village, dated to 5700 BC have been found, in works overseen by <a href="/w/index.php?title=Maria_Antonietta_Fugazzola&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Maria Antonietta Fugazzola (page does not exist)">Maria Antonietta Fugazzola Delpino</a>, director of the <a href="/wiki/Pigorini_National_Museum_of_Prehistory_and_Ethnography" title="Pigorini National Museum of Prehistory and Ethnography">Pigorini National Museum of Prehistory and Ethnography</a> in <a href="/wiki/Rome" title="Rome">Rome</a>, and president of the <a href="/w/index.php?title=Italian_Institute_of_Prehistory_and_Protohistory&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Italian Institute of Prehistory and Protohistory (page does not exist)">Italian Institute of Prehistory and Protohistory</a>. Thick oak pilings driven more than two meters into the <a href="/wiki/Subsoil" title="Subsoil">subsoil</a> have survived, thanks to <a href="/wiki/Anoxic_sea_water" class="mw-redirect" title="Anoxic sea water">anoxic</a> lake-bottom sediments; <a href="/wiki/Dendrochronology" title="Dendrochronology">dendrochronology</a> dates the settlement very accurately, for local tree-ring sequences have already been thoroughly established. The oldest post-Fugazzola Delpino has discovered at La Marmotta dates from around 5,690 BC, but she thinks ongoing work may yet reveal the village to have been born a century or so earlier. She is more certain of when it died: within a decade or so of 5230 BC. "Since the sixth millennium BC, as the climate has grown wetter, the water level in Lake Bracciano has risen more than 25 feet (8 m), and so the ruins of the Neolithic lakeshore village are now buried in bottom mud 400 yards (370 m) offshore".<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include" title="Wikipedia:Citing sources"><span title="A complete citation is needed. (November 2025)">full citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup>
</p><p>The strongest evidence that the Marmottans came from far away, probably originally making their way along the <a href="/wiki/Arrone_(river)" title="Arrone (river)">Arrone</a> to its source in Lake Bracciano, is simply that their culture was advanced. In the region around Lake Bracciano, according to Fugazzola Delpino, there is no sign of any but <a href="/wiki/Hunter-gatherer" title="Hunter-gatherer">hunter-gatherers</a> before the settlement was built at La Marmotta. The builders of the village had at their disposal, from the start, the entire "<a href="/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution" title="Neolithic Revolution">Neolithic package</a>": domesticated animals and plants, ceramic pots, polished stone tools, just as though they had unloaded all those things from their boats.
</p><p>They kept sheep and goats; they brought pigs and cows with them too, and two breeds of dog, and they planted a wide variety of crops— wheat and barley — and collected others in the woods. "They had everything", says Fugazzola, "They ate grains, vegetables, and also lots of fruit – apples, plums, raspberries, strawberries" Especially in winter they supplemented their diet with acorns, which they stored in large ceramic jars. They cultivated flax to make linen. They planted <a href="/wiki/Opium_poppy" class="mw-redirect" title="Opium poppy">opium poppies</a>" One of their boats has been recovered. A team of Czech archaeologists built a copy of the boat and sailed it 500 miles (800 km) along the Mediterranean coast to test its seaworthiness.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Roman_Era">Roman Era</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Lake_Bracciano&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Roman Era"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The area around the lake was always sparsely populated, but by the late Republic many waterfront villas had been built. The rich volcanic soil and the beautiful environment and views attracted rich patricians from Rome to build elaborate <i>villae rusticae</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Among these, <a href="/wiki/Imperial_Villa_of_Vicarello" title="Imperial Villa of Vicarello">Domitian's villa</a> with thermal spa at Vicarello was the ultimate example.
</p><p>However around the mid-1st century AD an environmental disaster occurred; the lake's water level rose, flooding the shore and many of the villas there more or less permanently.<sup id="cite_ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Legends">Legends</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Lake_Bracciano&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Legends"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The lake was originally known as "Lake Sabatine," named after the adjacent Sabatine Mountains and the ancient volcanic system in the area. It is believed that a prosperous city called "Sabate" once stood nearby. 
</p><p>In the period between the 16th and 17th centuries, a German historian and geographer documented in "<i>Italia Antiqua</i>" that the lake's calm waters occasionally revealed the submerged outlines of statues and edifices.
</p><p>Legend has it that <b>Sabate</b> was a flourishing city whose residents, however, strayed from ethical principles. In response, a deity unleashed a devastating catastrophe that obliterated the city, sparing only a virtuous young woman. She was instructed by the deity to flee and not look back. Pausing to rest where the small church of St. Maria del Riposo would later be built, the woman eventually glanced back towards Sabate. However, in place of the city, she saw a vast lake, which came to be known as Lake Sabatine or Lago di Bracciano (Lake Bracciano.) 
</p>

parsed_text:
Lake Bracciano ( Italian: Lago di Bracciano ) is a lake of volcanic origin in the Italian region of Lazio, 32 km (20 mi) northwest of Rome. It is the second largest lake in the region (second only to Lake Bolsena ) and one of the major lakes of Italy. It has a circular perimeter of approximately 32 km (20 mi). Its inflow is from precipitation runoff and percolation, and from underground springs, and its outflow is the Arrone.

The lake owes its origin to intense volcanic and tectonic activity from 600,000 to 40,000 years ago, which created many small volcanoes in the Sabatino territory. The main magma chamber was situated under the present lake of Bracciano. Its collapse created the depressed area now occupied by the lake, which is not a crater lake. Some small craters and calderas are still recognisable around the lake and in the immediate vicinity (Martignano, Baccano, Sacrofano).

Three towns border the lake, Bracciano, Anguillara Sabazia and Trevignano Romano.

The lake is an important tourist attraction. As it serves as a drinking water reservoir for the city of Rome, it has been under control since 1986 in order to avoid pollution of its waters. The use of motorboats is strictly forbidden (exceptions being made for a few professional fishermen and the authorities), and a centralised sewer system has been built for all the bordering towns in order to avoid any spoiling of the water quality, making Bracciano one of the cleanest lakes in Italy. The absence of motorized navigation favours sailing, canoeing and swimming.

In the last few years, the lake and its surroundings have been brought under further protection by the creation of a regional park, the Parco Regionale del complesso lacuale di Bracciano Martignano.

Water from the hills above the lake was transported to Rome by the Aqua Traiana, dedicated in 109 CE. The aqueduct was restored in the early 17th century by Pope Paul V, taking water to the Trastevere area of Rome and (via the Ponte Sisto ) to the Rione of Regola.

## History

### La Marmotta Neolithic settlement

At La Marmotta, a few hundred meters outside the village of Anguillara Sabazia, remains of an Early Neolithic lakeshore village, dated to 5700 BC have been found, in works overseen by Maria Antonietta Fugazzola Delpino, director of the Pigorini National Museum of Prehistory and Ethnography in Rome, and president of the Italian Institute of Prehistory and Protohistory. Thick oak pilings driven more than two meters into the subsoil have survived, thanks to anoxic lake-bottom sediments; dendrochronology dates the settlement very accurately, for local tree-ring sequences have already been thoroughly established. The oldest post-Fugazzola Delpino has discovered at La Marmotta dates from around 5,690 BC, but she thinks ongoing work may yet reveal the village to have been born a century or so earlier. She is more certain of when it died: within a decade or so of 5230 BC. "Since the sixth millennium BC, as the climate has grown wetter, the water level in Lake Bracciano has risen more than 25 feet (8 m), and so the ruins of the Neolithic lakeshore village are now buried in bottom mud 400 yards (370 m) offshore". [ full citation needed ]

The strongest evidence that the Marmottans came from far away, probably originally making their way along the Arrone to its source in Lake Bracciano, is simply that their culture was advanced. In the region around Lake Bracciano, according to Fugazzola Delpino, there is no sign of any but hunter-gatherers before the settlement was built at La Marmotta. The builders of the village had at their disposal, from the start, the entire " Neolithic package ": domesticated animals and plants, ceramic pots, polished stone tools, just as though they had unloaded all those things from their boats.

They kept sheep and goats; they brought pigs and cows with them too, and two breeds of dog, and they planted a wide variety of crops— wheat and barley — and collected others in the woods. "They had everything", says Fugazzola, "They ate grains, vegetables, and also lots of fruit – apples, plums, raspberries, strawberries" Especially in winter they supplemented their diet with acorns, which they stored in large ceramic jars. They cultivated flax to make linen. They planted opium poppies " One of their boats has been recovered. A team of Czech archaeologists built a copy of the boat and sailed it 500 miles (800 km) along the Mediterranean coast to test its seaworthiness.

### Roman Era

The area around the lake was always sparsely populated, but by the late Republic many waterfront villas had been built. The rich volcanic soil and the beautiful environment and views attracted rich patricians from Rome to build elaborate villae rusticae. Among these, Domitian's villa with thermal spa at Vicarello was the ultimate example.

However around the mid-1st century AD an environmental disaster occurred; the lake's water level rose, flooding the shore and many of the villas there more or less permanently.

### Legends

The lake was originally known as "Lake Sabatine," named after the adjacent Sabatine Mountains and the ancient volcanic system in the area. It is believed that a prosperous city called "Sabate" once stood nearby.

In the period between the 16th and 17th centuries, a German historian and geographer documented in " Italia Antiqua " that the lake's calm waters occasionally revealed the submerged outlines of statues and edifices.

Legend has it that Sabate was a flourishing city whose residents, however, strayed from ethical principles. In response, a deity unleashed a devastating catastrophe that obliterated the city, sparing only a virtuous young woman. She was instructed by the deity to flee and not look back. Pausing to rest where the small church of St. Maria del Riposo would later be built, the woman eventually glanced back towards Sabate. However, in place of the city, she saw a vast lake, which came to be known as Lake Sabatine or Lago di Bracciano (Lake Bracciano.)

<p><b>Società Sportiva Alba-Audace</b> was an <a href="/wiki/Italian_football" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian football">Italian football</a> club from the <a href="/wiki/Flaminio_(Rome)" title="Flaminio (Rome)">Flaminio</a> area of Rome, founded in 1907. The club is most noted for competing in the early <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship">Italian Football Championship</a> competitions, before becoming one of three Rome based clubs merging to form <a href="/wiki/AS_Roma" title="AS Roma">AS Roma</a> in 1927.<sup id="cite_ref-lastoria_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lastoria-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="History">History</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=SS_Alba-Audace_Roma&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: History"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The club was founded in 1907 as <b>Società Sportiva Alba</b> and was owned for its known existence by a Roman named Umberto Farneti. Farneti owned some land in the Flaminio area of Rome, this is where the club would play and consider their home.
</p><p>During the earliest days of the <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship">Italian Football Championship</a>, only <a href="/wiki/Northern_Italian" class="mw-redirect" title="Northern Italian">Northern Italian</a> football clubs gained entry into the league so Alba had to wait until <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1912-13#Lazio" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship 1912-13">1912–13</a> to make their championship debut. After qualifying for the Lazio section of the championship, Alba were forced to <a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/forfeit" class="extiw" title="wiktionary:forfeit">forfeit</a> their fixtures.
</p><p>At the end of <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1925-26" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship 1925-26">1925–26 season</a>, the club was forced by the Fascist Regime to absorb another Roman club, <b>Audace Roma</b>, but retained its colors. After only one season, Alba-Audace was merged with <a href="/wiki/Roman_F.C." class="mw-redirect" title="Roman F.C.">Roman F.C.</a> and <a href="/wiki/Fortitudo-Pro_Roma_S.G.S." class="mw-redirect" title="Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S.">Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S.</a> to form <a href="/wiki/AS_Roma" title="AS Roma">AS Roma</a>.
</p><p>The club briefly reformed in 1945 and, after merging with <b>Trastevere Roma</b>, took part to the <a href="/wiki/Serie_B_1946-47" class="mw-redirect" title="Serie B 1946-47">1946–47 Serie B</a> season as <b>Alba Trastevere</b> before disbanding in late 1960s.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Honours">Honours</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=SS_Alba-Audace_Roma&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Honours"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><b><a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship">Italian Football Championship</a>:</b>
</p>
<dl><dd><ul><li><b>Southern Champions</b>: <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1924-25" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship 1924-25">1924–25</a>; <a href="/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1925-26" class="mw-redirect" title="Italian Football Championship 1925-26">1925–26</a></li></ul></dd></dl>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Historical_names">Historical names</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=SS_Alba-Audace_Roma&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Historical names"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<ul><li>1907 – <b>S.S. Alba Roma</b> (<i>Società Sportiva Alba Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1926 – fusion with <b>Audace Roma</b> => <b>U.S. Alba-Audace Roma</b> (<i>Unione Sportiva Alba-Audace Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1927 – folded after merging with <a href="/wiki/Roman_F.C." class="mw-redirect" title="Roman F.C.">Roman F.C.</a> and <a href="/wiki/Fortitudo-Pro_Roma_S.G.S." class="mw-redirect" title="Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S.">Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S.</a></li>
<li>1930 – refounded as <b>A.S. Alba Roma</b> (<i>Associazione Sportiva Alba Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1940 – <b>A.S. Alba Motor Roma</b> (<i>Associazione Sportiva Alba Motor Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1942 – <b>A.S. Alba Roma</b> (<i>Associazione Sportiva Alba Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1944 – <b>Albaerotecnica Roma</b></li>
<li>1945 – <b>A.S. Albala Roma</b> (<i>Associazione Sportiva Albala Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1946 – fusion with <b><a href="/wiki/A.S.D._Trastevere_Calcio" class="mw-redirect" title="A.S.D. Trastevere Calcio">Trastevere Roma</a></b> => <b>U.S. Albalatrastevere Roma</b> (<i>Unione Sportiva Albalatrastevere Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1948 – <b>U.S. Albatrastevere Roma</b> (<i>Unione Sportiva Albatrastevere Roma</i>)</li>
<li>1968 – folded; refoundation of <a href="/wiki/A.S.D._Trastevere_Calcio" class="mw-redirect" title="A.S.D. Trastevere Calcio">Trastevere Roma</a></li></ul>

parsed_text:
Società Sportiva Alba-Audace was an Italian football club from the Flaminio area of Rome, founded in 1907. The club is most noted for competing in the early Italian Football Championship competitions, before becoming one of three Rome based clubs merging to form AS Roma in 1927.

## History

The club was founded in 1907 as Società Sportiva Alba and was owned for its known existence by a Roman named Umberto Farneti. Farneti owned some land in the Flaminio area of Rome, this is where the club would play and consider their home.

During the earliest days of the Italian Football Championship, only Northern Italian football clubs gained entry into the league so Alba had to wait until 1912–13 to make their championship debut. After qualifying for the Lazio section of the championship, Alba were forced to forfeit their fixtures.

At the end of 1925–26 season, the club was forced by the Fascist Regime to absorb another Roman club, Audace Roma, but retained its colors. After only one season, Alba-Audace was merged with Roman F.C. and Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S. to form AS Roma.

The club briefly reformed in 1945 and, after merging with Trastevere Roma, took part to the 1946–47 Serie B season as Alba Trastevere before disbanding in late 1960s.

## Honours

Italian Football Championship:

- Southern Champions : 1924–25 ; 1925–26

## Historical names

- 1907 – S.S. Alba Roma ( Società Sportiva Alba Roma )
- 1926 – fusion with Audace Roma => U.S. Alba-Audace Roma ( Unione Sportiva Alba-Audace Roma )
- 1927 – folded after merging with Roman F.C. and Fortitudo-Pro Roma S.G.S.
- 1930 – refounded as A.S. Alba Roma ( Associazione Sportiva Alba Roma )
- 1940 – A.S. Alba Motor Roma ( Associazione Sportiva Alba Motor Roma )
- 1942 – A.S. Alba Roma ( Associazione Sportiva Alba Roma )
- 1944 – Albaerotecnica Roma
- 1945 – A.S. Albala Roma ( Associazione Sportiva Albala Roma )
- 1946 – fusion with Trastevere Roma => U.S. Albalatrastevere Roma ( Unione Sportiva Albalatrastevere Roma )
- 1948 – U.S. Albatrastevere Roma ( Unione Sportiva Albatrastevere Roma )
- 1968 – folded; refoundation of Trastevere Roma

## Season-by-season record

Sources:

| Season | Division | Level | Position | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1912–13 | Prima Categoria Laziale | 1 | 6th | Do not advance to the final round |
| ... |
| 1914–15 | Promozione Laziale | 1 | 2nd | Do not advance to the final round |
| ... |
| 1920–21 | Promozione Laziale | 1 | 5th | Do not advance to the final round |
| 1921–22 | Lega Sud Laziale | 1 | 2nd | Do not advance to the final round |
| 1922–23 | Lega Sud, Semifinals Gr. A | 1 | 2nd | Do not advance to the final round |
| 1923–24 | Lega Sud, Finals | 1 | 2nd | Do not advance to the final round |
| 1924–25 | Lega Sud, Finals | 1 | 1st | Lost in national finals to Bologna |
| 1925–26 | Lega Sud, Finals | 1 | 1st | Lost in national finals to Juventus |
| 1926–27 | Divisione Nazionale, Gr. A | 1 | 9th | Disbanded |
| ... |
| 1939–40 | Serie C, Gr. G | 3 | 13th |  |
| 1940–41 | Serie C, Gr. G | 3 | 5th |  |
| 1941–42 | Serie C, Gr. E | 3 | 2nd |  |
| 1942–43 | Serie C, Gr. I | 3 | 2nd |  |
| ... |
| 1945–46 | Serie C, Gr. South C | 3 | 1st | Promotion |
| 1946–47 | Serie B, Gr. C | 2 | 14th | Relegated |
| 1947–48 | Serie C, Gr. Central E | 3 | 7th | Relegated |
